Philip N. Jefferson: Philip N. Jefferson is the Vice Chair of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, having taken office on September 13, 2023, for a four-year term. He has been a member of the Board since May 23, 2022, filling an unexpired term that extends to January 31, 2036. Prior to his current role, Dr. Jefferson served as vice president for academic affairs and dean of faculty at Davidson College, where he was also the Paul B. Freeland Professor of Economics. He previously held the position of chair of the Department of Economics at Swarthmore College and was the Centennial Professor of Economics there. Earlier in his career, Dr. Jefferson worked as an economist at the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System. He has also been president of the National Economic Association and served on the Vassar College Board of Trustees, as well as the Board of Advisors of the Opportunity and Inclusive Growth Institute at the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis. Dr. Jefferson earned a BA in economics from Vassar College and both an MA and a PhD in economics from the University of Virginia.
Michael S. Barr: Michael S. Barr has been a member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System since July 19, 2022, and serves as the Vice Chair for Supervision until February 28, 2025. Before his appointment, he was the Joan and Sanford Weill Dean of the Gerald R. Ford School of Public Policy at the University of Michigan, where he also held the positions of Frank Murphy Collegiate Professor of Public Policy and Roy F. and Jean Humphrey Proffitt Professor of Law. Barr founded and directed the Center on Finance, Law & Policy at the University of Michigan, teaching courses on financial regulation and international finance. His prior government experience includes serving as the assistant secretary for financial institutions at the U.S. Department of the Treasury from 2009 to 2010 and various roles under President Bill Clinton, including special assistant to the Treasury Secretary and adviser on the policy planning staff at the U.S. Department of State. Barr also clerked for U.S. Supreme Court Justice David H. Souter. He earned a BA in history from Yale University, an MPhil in international relations from Oxford University, and a JD from Yale Law School.
Lisa D. Cook: Lisa D. Cook is a member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, having taken office on May 23, 2022, to fill an unexpired term ending January 31, 2024. She was reappointed on September 8, 2023, and sworn in for a new term ending January 31, 2038. Before her appointment, Dr. Cook was a professor of economics and international relations at Michigan State University and served as director of the American Economic Association Summer Training Program from 2018 to 2021. She has also been a research associate at the National Bureau of Economic Research and held faculty positions at Harvard University's Kennedy School of Government, where she was deputy director for Africa Research at the Center for International Development. Dr. Cook's government experience includes serving as a senior economist on the Council of Economic Advisers under President Barack Obama and as a senior adviser on finance and development in the U.S. Department of Treasury's Office of International Affairs. She holds a BA in philosophy from Spelman College, a second BA in philosophy, politics, and economics from Oxford University as a Marshall Scholar, and a PhD in economics from the University of California, Berkeley.
Christopher J. Waller: Christopher J. Waller has served as a member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System since December 18, 2020, with his term set to expire on January 31, 2030. Before his appointment to the Board, Dr. Waller was the executive vice president and director of research at the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, a position he held since 2009. His academic career includes serving as a professor and the Gilbert F. Schaefer Chair of Economics at the University of Notre Dame, where he was also a research fellow at the Kellogg Institute for International Studies. Prior to that, he was a professor and held the Carol Martin Gatton Chair of Macroeconomics and Monetary Economics at the University of Kentucky from 1998 to 2003, during which he was a research fellow at the Center for European Integration Studies at the University of Bonn. Dr. Waller's early career included roles at Indiana University, where he was the director of graduate studies in the Department of Economics. He holds a BS in economics from Bemidji State University and both an MA and PhD from Washington State University.
Mary C. Daly: Mary C. Daly is the President and Chief Executive Officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, where she plays a pivotal role in shaping monetary policy for the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. She joined the San Francisco Fed in 1996, previously serving as the executive vice president and director of research. Daly has provided her expertise as an advisor to several prominent organizations, including the Congressional Budget Office and the Social Security Administration, and has held visiting professor positions at Cornell University and the University of California, Davis. She earned her Ph.D. in economics from Syracuse University, an M.S. from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, and a B.A. from the University of Missouri-Kansas City. Additionally, she completed a post-doctoral fellowship at Northwestern University and is a research affiliate at the IZA Institute of Labor Economics. Daly's research focuses on employment trends, economic growth, and the Federal Reserve's dual mandate of maximum employment and price stability. Her personal journey, which includes dropping out of high school at 15 to support her family, informs her commitment to economic opportunity for all Americans.
Thomas I. Barkin: Thomas I. Barkin, born in 1961 in Tampa, Florida, is the eighth president and chief executive officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond, a role he has held since January 2018. He is also a member of the Federal Open Market Committee. Barkin earned his Bachelor of Arts in economics from Harvard University in 1983, followed by a joint Juris Doctor and Master of Business Administration in 1987. Before joining the Federal Reserve, he had a distinguished career at McKinsey & Company, where he spent three decades, serving as chief financial officer from 2009 to 2015 and chief risk officer from 2015 to 2017. In his most recent position as a senior partner in McKinsey's Atlanta office, he specialized in advising financial institutions as well as travel and transportation companies. Barkin has also contributed to the community through his service on the Emory University Board of Trustees and the executive board of the United States Golf Association.
Susan M. Collins: Susan M. Collins is the President and Chief Executive Officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of Boston, a position she has held since July 2022. In this role, she participates in the Federal Open Market Committee, contributing to the monetary policy decisions of the United States. Collins has a distinguished background as an international macroeconomist, with extensive research focusing on economic growth determinants, exchange rate regimes, and the effects of global integration on U.S. labor markets. She began her academic career at Harvard University before moving to Georgetown University, where she served as a professor of economics and a senior fellow at the Brookings Institution. Prior to her current role, Collins spent 15 years at the University of Michigan, where she was provost and executive vice president for academic affairs and held the Edward M. Gramlich Collegiate Professorship. She has also served on the Board of Directors of the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago and has been recognized as a Distinguished Fellow by the American Economic Association for 2025. Collins earned her doctorate in economics from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and her bachelor's degree in economics from Harvard University.
Beth M. Hammack: Beth M. Hammack is the president and chief executive officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland, a position she has held since August 21, 2024. In her role, she is actively involved in shaping U.S. monetary policy and manages a workforce of 1,100 employees across the Bank's offices in Cleveland, Cincinnati, and Pittsburgh, focusing on economic research, banking supervision, and payment services. Prior to her current position, Hammack had a distinguished career in various leadership roles within the Federal Reserve System, contributing her expertise to numerous economic and policy discussions. She is dedicated to engaging with communities throughout the Fourth Federal Reserve District, which includes Ohio, western Pennsylvania, eastern Kentucky, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia, to gather insights on economic conditions. Hammack is known for her public speaking and media engagements, where she articulates her perspectives on economic issues. Her educational background and previous experiences have equipped her with a deep understanding of the financial landscape, enabling her to effectively represent regional interests in national monetary policy conversations.
Jeffrey R. Schmid: Jeffrey R. Schmid currently serves as the president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, a role he has held since August 21, 2023, succeeding Esther L. George. Schmid has a robust background in banking, having served as chairman and CEO of Mutual of Omaha Bank from 2007 to 2019, where he was instrumental in establishing the bank, which grew to nearly 2,000 employees. After the bank's sale to CIT Group in 2019, he took on the role of CEO at Susser Bank, a family-owned institution in Dallas. Before his appointment at the Kansas City Fed, Schmid led the Southwestern Graduate School of Banking at Southern Methodist University starting in 2021. He began his career as a field examiner at the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC). Schmid holds a bachelor's degree in business administration from the University of Nebraska-Lincoln and is also a graduate of the Southwestern Graduate School of Banking.
Austan D. Goolsbee: Austan D. Goolsbee is the president and chief executive officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago, a role he has held since January 2023. In this position, he serves on the Federal Open Market Committee, contributing to the monetary policymaking process while overseeing the Chicago Fed's research, economic monitoring, banking supervision, and financial services. Before joining the Chicago Fed, Goolsbee was the Robert P. Gwinn Professor of Economics at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business, where he began his academic career in 1995 and gained recognition for his empirical research on various industries and economic policies. His previous government experience includes serving as a member and chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers from 2009 to 2011, during which he was part of the President’s cabinet. Goolsbee has also held positions on several advisory boards, including the Economic Advisory Panel to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. He earned his PhD in economics from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and holds both a BA and MA in economics from Yale University. He is married and has three children.
Anna Paulson: Anna Paulson is the 12th president and chief executive officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia, a position she has held since July 1, 2025. In this role, she serves on the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), which is responsible for shaping the nation's monetary policy, and oversees various Bank activities, including economic research, banking supervision, community outreach, and payment services. With over 20 years of experience in policy analysis and research focused on financial stability and monetary policy, Paulson previously served as executive vice president and director of research at the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago. There, she acted as a senior advisor to the Chicago Fed's president and CEO and managed the department that supports monetary policymaking through research on banking, macroeconomics, and regional economies. Paulson's expertise extends to financial markets and institutions, particularly the insurance industry, and her research has been published in prominent scholarly journals. She earned her Ph.D. in economics from the University of Chicago and holds a B.A. from Carleton College. Prior to her role at the Chicago Fed, she was an assistant professor of finance at the Kellogg School of Management and a postdoctoral fellow at Princeton University.
Michelle W. Bowman: Michelle W. Bowman has been a member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System since November 26, 2018, initially appointed to fill an unexpired term and later reappointed for a full 14-year term ending January 31, 2034. On June 9, 2025, she was sworn in as the third Vice Chair for Supervision, overseeing the regulation of depository institution holding companies and other financial firms. As the only Board member with banking and state supervisory experience, she chairs the Subcommittee on Smaller and Regional Community Banking and serves on the Payments, Clearing, and Settlement Committee. Additionally, Bowman chairs the Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council and holds international roles with the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision and the Financial Stability Board. Prior to her current role, she was the Kansas state bank commissioner from January 2017 to November 2018 and vice president of Farmers & Drovers Bank from 2010 to 2017. Her public service includes positions with the Department of Homeland Security and the Federal Emergency Management Agency, as well as working for Senator Bob Dole. Bowman earned her bachelor's degree from the University of Kansas and a JD from Washburn University School of Law, and she is a member of the New York State Bar.
Lorie K. Logan: Lorie K. Logan is the 14th president and chief executive officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, having assumed office on August 22, 2022. In her role, she oversees a workforce of nearly 1,400 employees across the Eleventh Federal Reserve District, which includes Texas, northern Louisiana, and southern New Mexico. As a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee, she plays a critical role in shaping U.S. monetary policy. Logan has a distinguished career at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, where she spent over two decades in the Markets Group, including serving as manager of the System Open Market Account and as an executive vice president. She is recognized as a leading expert on the Federal Reserve’s balance sheet and was instrumental in developing strategies to address systemic risks during the COVID-19 pandemic and the Global Financial Crisis. Logan holds a bachelor’s degree in political science from Davidson College and a master’s in public administration from Columbia University. She is actively involved in various organizations, including the Council on Foreign Relations and the United Way of Metropolitan Dallas, and is originally from Versailles, Kentucky.
John C. Williams: John C. Williams is the president and chief executive officer of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, a position he has held since June 2018. In this capacity, he leads one of the twelve Reserve Banks that comprise the Federal Reserve System and serves as vice chair and a permanent voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee, which is responsible for the nation’s monetary policy. Prior to his current role, Williams was the president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco from 2011 to mid-2018, and he began his career in 1994 as an economist at the Board of Governors. He also served as a senior economist at the White House Council of Economic Advisers and was a lecturer at Stanford University’s Graduate School of Business. Williams holds a PhD in economics from Stanford University, a master’s degree from the London School of Economics, and a bachelor’s degree from the University of California at Berkeley. He is known for his advocacy of policies to stimulate the economy and has conducted significant research on monetary policy issues, including the Zero Lower Bound and the neutral rate of interest. Williams is originally from California and currently resides in New York with his wife and two sons.
Jerome H. Powell: Jerome H. Powell has served as a member of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System since May 25, 2012, and was reappointed for a term ending January 31, 2028. He has held the position of chair of the Board and chair of the Federal Open Market Committee, the principal monetary policymaking body of the Federal Reserve, since February 5, 2018. Before his appointment to the Board, Powell was a visiting scholar at the Bipartisan Policy Center, focusing on fiscal issues. He was a partner at The Carlyle Group from 1997 to 2005 and previously served as Assistant Secretary and Under Secretary of the U.S. Department of the Treasury under President George H.W. Bush, where he managed policy on financial institutions and the Treasury debt market. Powell began his career as a lawyer and investment banker in New York City. He holds an AB in politics from Princeton University and a law degree from Georgetown University, where he was editor-in-chief of the Georgetown Law Journal. Born in February 1953 in Washington, D.C., he is married and has three children.
Kevin M. Warsh: Kevin M. Warsh is the chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, having taken office on May 22, 2026, for a four-year term. He also chairs the Federal Open Market Committee and is a member of the Board of Governors, with his current term set to end on January 31, 2040. Warsh previously served on the Board from 2006 to 2011. After his initial tenure, he became the Shepard Family Distinguished Visiting Fellow in Economics at Stanford University's Hoover Institution and a lecturer at the Stanford Graduate School of Business, while also working as a partner at Duquesne Family Office. Prior to his time at the Federal Reserve, he was a special assistant to the president for economic policy and executive secretary of the National Economic Council from 2002 to 2006, and he was a member of the President's Working Group on Financial Markets. Warsh began his career at Morgan Stanley, where he worked from 1995 to 2002 as vice president and executive director. He holds an AB from Stanford University and a JD from Harvard Law School.
Neel Kashkari: Neel Kashkari has served as the president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis since January 1, 2016. In this capacity, he is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee, contributing the Ninth District's insights to national monetary policy discussions. Kashkari oversees the Bank's operations, including supervision, regulation, and payments services, and has spearheaded initiatives such as the Opportunity & Inclusive Growth Institute and the Center for Indian Country Development. His leadership has also focused on addressing the issue of "too big to fail" banks through proposed regulatory reforms. Prior to his role at the Minneapolis Fed, Kashkari began his career as an aerospace engineer at TRW, developing technology for NASA. He later worked at Goldman Sachs, assisting technology firms with capital raising and strategic transactions. From 2006 to 2009, he held senior positions at the U.S. Department of the Treasury, including assistant secretary, where he managed the Troubled Asset Relief Program during the financial crisis. Kashkari earned his bachelor's and master's degrees in mechanical engineering from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign and an MBA from the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania.
